text: Apple reported record quarterly revenue of $123.9 billion, up 11 percent year over year.
The SEC is investigating Tesla over its self-driving claims.
Microsoft to acquire Activision Blizzard for $68.7 billion.
Apple sued Samsung over patent violations.
Nvidia reported record data center revenue of $22.6 billion, beating analyst estimates by a wide margin.
Intel lost a key server CPU contract to AMD at a major US cloud provider.
The DOJ filed an antitrust lawsuit against Google over its dominance in the online search advertising market.
Tesla laid off 10 percent of its global workforce as the company moved to cut costs amid slowing EV demand.
Pfizer received FDA approval for its new RSV vaccine targeting adults over 60.
Bed Bath and Beyond filed for Chapter 11 bankruptcy protection, citing unsustainable debt and declining sales.

Also dragging on spirits was disappointment over Amazon's earnings released late Thursday. The performance of its AWS cloud unit failed to live up to lofty expectations set by rivals Google and Microsoft, sending its shares down over 8%. But Apple stock rose after its results beat expectations, boosted by surprisingly strong iPhone sales.
Semiconductor stocks diverged sharply after earnings. Nvidia surged on record AI chip demand while Texas Instruments fell after warning of a prolonged inventory correction in industrial markets.
Boeing's delivery numbers fell well short of the benchmark set by Airbus, which had posted its strongest quarterly output in five years.
OpenAI hired three prominent AI safety researchers from DeepMind, strengthening its alignment team ahead of its next model release.

The results were mixed across the social media sector. While Meta reported strong advertising growth of 27 percent, Pinterest missed estimates and Snap warned of slowing demand from brand advertisers.
Apple launched its first generative AI features across iPhone and Mac, positioning Apple Intelligence as a privacy-first alternative to ChatGPT.
Meta was fined 1.3 billion euros by Irish regulators for transferring European user data to US servers in violation of GDPR.
Goldman Sachs announced it would exit its consumer banking business entirely, taking a 4 billion dollar writedown on Marcus as it pivoted back to investment banking.
Musk's rocket company secured a 2.9 billion dollar NASA contract for its Starship vehicle to serve as the lunar lander for the Artemis program.
The chipmaker's shares fell 12 percent after it warned that data center customers were pausing orders to reassess their AI infrastructure needs following a period of aggressive overbuilding.
Trump's sweeping tariffs on Chinese imports rattled equity markets and sent technology stocks sharply lower.
Rising input costs and weak consumer demand dragged on Procter & Gamble's quarterly margins.
Visa topped analyst estimates for the quarter, driven by resilient cross-border transaction volumes.
Pfizer agreed to acquire oncology biotech Seagen in a $43 billion all-cash deal.
The FTC sued Amazon for illegally maintaining monopoly power in the online retail market.
Rite Aid filed for Chapter 11 bankruptcy protection, weighed down by opioid lawsuit settlements and mounting debt.
Google hired a prominent machine learning researcher away from OpenAI to lead its Gemini research team.
Wall Street analysts raised concerns about the pace of rate cuts heading into the second half of the year.
Amazon reported strong AWS growth but warned that capital expenditure would surge significantly next year.
The EU fined Apple 1.8 billion euros for abusing its dominant position in the music streaming market.
China imposed retaliatory tariffs on U.S. agricultural exports, escalating the trade dispute.
Japan's central bank raised interest rates for the first time in seventeen years, sending the yen sharply higher.
Iran and the U.S. traded strikes over control of the Strait of Hormuz, a critical route for global oil shipments.
